subject: Physics
authority: Ministry of Education - Republic of Liberia
description: The Physics syllabus provides learners with sound knowledge of fundamental concepts in Physics, using a conceptual approach that focuses on scientific phenomena, facts, concepts, theories, laws and principles.
general_objectives: Demonstrate comprehension of the basic principles and applications of Physics
Exhibit scientific skills, competencies and attitudes necessary for the advancement of Physics
Recognize the usefulness as well as limitations of the scientific method
primary_texts: Brian Arnold, Steve Wolley & Penny Johnson, Edexcel IGCSE Physics (Pearson, 2009)
John Motey Addo & Barry Jackson, Senior High Physics (Longman, 2009)
Peter Asiedu, Physics for Senior High Schools in West Africa - Aki-Ola Series (Millennium Edition 4-2011)
M. Melkon, Principles of Physics (8th Edition)
Paul Attakorah, Atta Kay Physics (Vol. 1 & 2, 2012 Edition)
M. W. Anyakoha, New School Physics for Senior Secondary Schools (2016 Edition)
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)

periods.topic: Introduction to Physics and Properties of Matter
specific_objectives: Discuss the importance of Physics
Identify scalar and vector quantities
Identify basic mathematical concepts (scientific notation, significant figures)
Distinguish between fundamental and derived physical quantities and their units
Analyze dimensional analysis in terms of the relationship between fundamental and derived quantities
Measure with various measuring instruments
Distinguish between density and relative density
content_outline: Development of Physics: branches, basic mathematical concepts
Measurement: systems of measurement, fundamental quantities and units, derived quantities and units, metric prefixes and conversion, significant figures, accuracy and precision, errors in measurement
Pressure in solids, liquids, and gases
Dimensional analysis and its use in physics
Measuring instruments: meter rule, vernier caliper, micrometer screw gauge, beam balance, spring balance, stop watches, electronic balance, thermometer
Scalar and vector quantities: definitions, classification of quantities as scalars and vectors
suggested_activities: Calculation and deriving of units
Group discussion on the importance of studying Physics
Discuss branches of Physics
Discuss basic mathematical concepts (scientific notation, significant figures, unit conversion)
Discuss fundamental and derived quantities with their SI units
Use dimensional analysis to derive relationships between quantities
Distinguish scalar and vector quantities
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Use locally available measuring tools. Relate density to everyday items like palm oil, water, and rubber. Connect pressure concepts to traditional palm wine tapping, carrying loads on the head (pressure on surfaces), and tire inflation.

specific_objectives: Analyze motion (uniformly accelerated)
Apply the basic equations of motion and interpret motion graphs
Use Newton's laws of motion and analyze the effect of gravity on falling bodies
Discuss the force of gravitation between objects
content_outline: Nature and types of motion: distance and displacement, speed, velocity and acceleration
Uniformly accelerated motion: uniform acceleration, equations of uniform linear motion
Graphical analysis of uniform motion: position-time graphs, displacement-time graphs, velocity-time graphs
suggested_activities: Discuss using examples to distinguish distance, displacement, speed, velocity, acceleration, uniform velocity, instantaneous velocity and average velocity
Calculate displacement and acceleration of a moving object with respect to time
Derive equations of uniformly accelerated motion to solve problems
Draw and interpret graphs illustrating uniformly accelerated motion
Demonstrate motion of freely falling bodies due to gravity
Demonstrate Newton's Laws of Motion
Hammering nails to demonstrate Newton's Third Law of Motion
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Use local examples: a car traveling from Monrovia to Buchanan (distance vs displacement), a football kicked on a field (projectile), dropping a coconut from a palm tree (free fall), pushing a wheelbarrow (Newton's laws). Relate gravity to why ripe mangoes fall from trees.

specific_objectives: Identify the characteristics of work, energy and power and their SI units
Solve simple problems involving work, energy (potential and kinetic) and power
Distinguish the types of simple machines
Distinguish between work input and work output
Compute potential and kinetic energies problems
Demonstrate the law of conservation of mechanical energy and its application
content_outline: Work, Energy and Power: kinds of energy (potential and kinetic), conservation of energy (transformation)
Machines: kinds of simple machines, mechanical advantage, velocity ratios and efficiency of machines
suggested_activities: Examine characteristics of work, energy, and power
Analyze the system of pulleys and inclined plane
Discover simple machines at home and in school
Display Hooke's law in relationship to Young's modulus
Analyze potential and kinetic energies
Discuss the Law of Conservation of mechanical energy and relate it to energy changes
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Connect to local life: women carrying water on their heads (work against gravity), using a lever to move heavy logs, the inclined planes used in construction, bicycle mechanics (gears as simple machines), the energy in flowing rivers and potential for hydroelectric power like the Mt. Coffee plant.

specific_objectives: Distinguish between temperature and heat
Outline the steps and principles involved in the measurement of temperature
Describe the features and use of different types of thermometers
Identify fundamental intervals of thermometers
Solve simple thermometry problems
Demonstrate Charles's, Boyle's, Combined gas laws
content_outline: Temperature and heat: difference between temperature and heat
Temperature scales: Fahrenheit, Celsius, and Kelvin conversions
Thermometry: types of thermometers and their properties, calibration of thermometers
Gas Laws: Charles's Law, Boyle's Law, Combined Gas Law, Pressure Law
suggested_activities: Bring out the concept of heat and temperature
Distinguish between heat and temperature
Discuss the Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin scales and their relationships
Solve problems associated with Charles's, Boyle's, Combined gas, and Pressure Laws
Use syringes to demonstrate pressure laws
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Relate heat to cooking over coal pots and three-stone fires. Explain thermal expansion using the expansion of zinc roofing sheets in the hot sun. Use the local climate (tropical heat) to discuss temperature measurement. Boyle's law can be demonstrated with locally available syringes from health clinics.

specific_objectives: Identify the two kinds of electric charges
Distinguish between conductors and insulators
State the basic and Coulomb's Laws of Electrostatics
Illustrate lines of force relative to electric charges
Explain/discuss the methods of charging a body
Solve problems on Coulomb's Law, electric field intensity and work done in an electric field
Identify/describe the types of capacitors and their uses
Discuss the relationship between potential difference and capacitance
Solve problems on networks of capacitors
content_outline: Network of capacitors: series, parallel, series-parallel
suggested_activities: Examine the two charges of electrostatic in an electric field
Examine electrification using silk and glass rod
Calculate applying the equation of Coulomb's Law
Experiments involving charge and discharge of capacitors
Calculations involving electric field intensity, potential difference and gradients
Design the structure of capacitors
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Relate static electricity to the shock felt when touching a car door in dry season, or rubbing a balloon on hair. Discuss how lightning during rainy season is a massive electrostatic discharge. Connect capacitors to the energy storage in phone chargers and solar battery systems common in off-grid Liberian communities.

specific_objectives: Distinguish between atoms and molecules
Examine the kinetic theory of matter
Distinguish between cohesion and adhesion
Analyze Hooke's law and apply Young's modulus in solving problems
content_outline: Elasticity and Hooke's Law (Young's/Elastic modulus) with graphical representation
suggested_activities: Distinguish between atoms and molecules
Discuss properties of matter
Discuss viscosity and diffusion in solid, liquid and gas
Work with springs and elastic strings
Discuss the phases of matter
Experiment using test tube hydrometer
Demonstrate cohesion and adhesion
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Use local materials: rubber bands and slingshots (elasticity/Hooke's law), palm oil and water for viscosity comparison, soap bubbles for surface tension, diffusion of perfume or cooking smells in the classroom. Relate states of matter to local observations: morning dew (condensation), boiling cassava (evaporation), ice from a freezer.

specific_objectives: Add and resolve vectors
Solve problems on simple harmonic motion
Use the equations of motion in solving one and two dimensions problems
Distinguish the types of motion (projectile, rotational, circular, simple harmonic and oscillatory) and their applications
suggested_activities: Analyze vectors in one and two dimensions
Calculate the dimension of vectors in one and two dimensions
Examine the equations of motion to solve exercises
Demonstrate types of motion: projectile, rotational, circular, simple harmonic, oscillatory
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Projectile motion: throwing a stone across a creek, a football kicked at an angle. Circular motion: the spinning of a traditional grinding stone, a car going around a roundabout in Monrovia. Simple harmonic motion: a child on a swing, the pendulum of a grandfather clock at the National Museum.

specific_objectives: Combine force vectors to produce resultant force
Use the cosine and sine laws in the resolution of force
Distinguish between resultant and equilibrant force
Demonstrate the two conditions for equilibrium
Distinguish the types of friction and calculate its coefficient
Distinguish between center of gravity and center of mass
content_outline: Friction: types, coefficient, methods of reducing friction
suggested_activities: Construct vectors with magnitudes in the same and opposite directions to find the resultant
Construct a vector with a magnitude and direction in a plane and compute its components
Demonstrate gravity and equilibrium experiments using force table
Discuss coefficient of kinetic and static frictions on the inclined plane
Using pencils as rollers to overcome friction of a block of wood
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Friction: why vehicles get stuck on muddy roads in rainy season vs. paved roads. Equilibrium: balancing loads on the head (center of gravity), how market women balance large trays of goods. Torque: opening a door, using a wrench to tighten a bolt on a generator.

specific_objectives: Analyze the nature and the effect of momentum
Differentiate between impulse and momentum
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions
Distinguish between the laws of conservation of linear and angular momentum and their applications
content_outline: Nature of linear momentum and impulse
suggested_activities: Discuss the nature and effect of momentum and impulse
Construct experiment verifying conservation of momentum using pendulum
Construct the steps (equations) involving calculations of elastic and inelastic collision
Design momentum carts to display conservation of momentum
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Momentum: why a moving truck is harder to stop than a moving bicycle, the impact of a football header. Impulse: why you bend your knees when jumping from a height, why car bumpers crumple in accidents. Collisions: two marbles hitting each other (elastic), two lumps of clay sticking together (inelastic).

specific_objectives: Elaborate the concept and characteristics of heat
Distinguish between specific heat and specific heat capacity
Distinguish between the methods of heat transfer and the laws of heat exchange
Discuss heat transfer in a vacuum flask
content_outline: Specific heat and specific heat capacity
Heat transfer and the laws of heat exchange
Latent heat of fusion and vaporization
suggested_activities: Discuss the characteristics of heat
Conduct calorimeter heat experiments
Estimate and calculate specific heat and specific heat capacity of an object
Examine the equation and calculate the latent heat of fusion and heat of vaporization
Demonstrate the principles of combustion engines
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Heat transfer: why metal cooking pots get hot (conduction), how heat rises from a coal pot (convection), feeling the sun's warmth (radiation). Latent heat: why sweating cools you down, why clothes dry on a line. Vacuum flask: how a thermos keeps food warm for market women. Specific heat: why sand on the beach is hotter than the ocean water.

specific_objectives: Analyze characteristics, concept and components of waves
Elaborate on the properties and the categories of waves
Design methods of production and transmission of sound wave and its application
Compute the speed of sound relative to its temperature
Analyze the Doppler Effect
Distinguish between loudness and intensity of sound, intensity and intensity level, music and noise, stringed and non-stringed instruments
Analyze vibrations in strings and tubes (pipes)
content_outline: Nature, characteristics and properties of waves
Sound waves: production, characteristics and transmission
suggested_activities: Discuss the nature of waves and its components
Discuss wave characteristics: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, polarization
Examine the wave equation (v = λf)
Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves using string and coil spring
Demonstrate Doppler effect using tuning forks
Use percussion instruments (drum, bells, cymbal, horn, guitar, flute, sassa) to produce different sounds
Production and transmission of sound in tubes (closed and open)
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Waves: ripples in a pond or river, ocean waves on Liberian beaches. Sound: the sassa and drums used in traditional music, church bells, the call to prayer. Doppler effect: the changing pitch of a car horn as it passes on Tubman Boulevard. Resonance: blowing across the mouth of an empty bottle. Echo: shouting in a large concrete building.

specific_objectives: Determine the sources and importance of radiant energy
Construct the linear propagation of light indicating shadows and eclipses
Analyze the major regions of the electromagnetic spectrum, the photoelectric effect and principle of a laser
Examine the effects of burning on the environment
Analyze and demonstrate image formation using a mirror
content_outline: Propagation of light: shadows and eclipses, pin-hole camera
Reflection of light by plane and spherical mirrors
suggested_activities: Discuss the sources of light and its production
Distinguish between shadow and eclipse
Using torch light and copybook, and torch light and ball to demonstrate shadows and eclipses
Construct a ray box to indicate rectilinear propagation of light rays
Discuss the properties of luminous and non-luminous objects
Discuss the nature and types of mirrors
Construct ray diagrams displaying image formation by mirror
Apply mirror equation to calculate mirror problems
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Light: the sun as the primary light source, kerosene lamps and candles used in areas without electricity. Shadows: how shadows change throughout the day, shadow puppets. Mirrors: side mirrors on vehicles, reflecting sunlight with a mirror to signal. Electromagnetic spectrum: radio waves (ELBC, Radio Liberia), UV rays and sunburn, X-rays at JFK hospital.

specific_objectives: Analyze and justify the laws of refraction
Calculate the refractive index of various materials
Apply Snell's law equation to compute the index of refraction
Distinguish between primary colors and primary pigments
Distinguish between total internal reflection and critical angle
content_outline: Refraction at plane interface: laws of refraction
Refractive index: Snell's Law, speed of light, real depth and apparent depth, critical angle and total internal reflection
Critical angle and total internal reflection
suggested_activities: Discuss the law of refraction
Discuss optical instruments (lens, magnifying glass, prism glass, torch light on water in plastic)
Use glass block and prism to create the refractive index of material
Discuss how light ray leaves water to create critical angle
Discuss eye defects and correction, the camera, projection, simple and compound microscopes, and telescopes
Use bending pencil in the glass of water to practice refraction
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Refraction: why a stick looks bent in water (washing in a basin), why the pool appears shallower than it is. Dispersion: rainbow after rain. Lenses: eyeglasses, magnifying glasses, the camera on a phone. Total internal reflection: fiber optic cables (relate to modern telecommunications coming to Liberia), the sparkle of a diamond.

specific_objectives: Identify sources of direct current
Distinguish and analyze series and parallel circuits
Analyze Ohm's Law and its uses in circuit analysis
Formulate Kirchhoff's laws of electric energy and its application in circuits
Calculate internal resistance of cells/battery
content_outline: Sources of direct current: primary and secondary cells, fuel and solar cells
Resistance and Resistivity: Ohm's Law, factors affecting the resistance of the conductor
Electrical energy and power: cost of electrical energy
suggested_activities: Discuss the process in which direct currents are produced
Construct and analyze series, parallel, and combination circuits using lamps and dry cells
Verification of Ohm's Law through lab experiment
Construct practical circuit diagram to apply Ohm's law
Discuss the importance of ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter in circuit analysis
Demonstrate heating effect in circuits using cells and light bulb
periods.local_contextualization_notes: DC electricity: dry cell batteries used in torches and radios, solar panels on homes and schools, car batteries. Series vs parallel: why Christmas lights all go out when one burns out (series) vs house wiring (parallel). Cost of electrical energy: understanding LEC electricity bills, why generators are expensive to run. Relate to solar energy systems increasingly used across Liberia.

specific_objectives: Analyze the origin of magnets
Compare the relationship between electricity and magnetism
Distinguish motor from generator
Elaborate the principle of transformer and its function
Distinguish between AC and DC motors
content_outline: Magnets: types and properties, magnetic materials, concept of magnetic fields, methods of magnetizing and demagnetizing magnets
Magnetic Fields: types, magnetic flux and magnetic flux density, force in a magnetic field, moving coil galvanometer
Electromagnetic Induction: laws of electromagnetic induction, induced current and EMF, inductors, generators, motors and transformers
suggested_activities: Discuss the origin of magnets and their importance
Distinguish the various types of magnets and their applications
Discuss the relationship between electricity and magnetism
Construct a diagram showing the conversion process between AC to DC motor
Construct and analyze diagrams of motor and generator
Verify the transformer equation (Vp/Vs = Np/Ns) to calculate input or output
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Magnets: compass needles pointing north, fridge magnets, magnetic catches on doors. Electromagnetic induction: how generators (common in Liberia due to power outages) produce electricity, how transformers step voltage up and down for power distribution. Motors: electric fans, water pumps. Relate to the national power grid and the importance of transformers in bringing electricity to communities.

specific_objectives: Identify electrical measuring instruments and state their uses
Demonstrate the functions of alternating current
Distinguish between resistance and impedance
Analyze the R, C, L circuits and the phase diagram of RCL circuits
Analyze the principle of the cathode ray tube and applications of transistors and diodes
content_outline: Basic Electronics: semi-conductors (doping, intrinsic and extrinsic, P-type and N-type, band theory)
suggested_activities: Discuss electrical measuring instruments for verification
Discuss vacuum tubes
Perform experiments in circuits involving inductor, capacitor, resistor
Demonstrate the uses of transistors and diodes in a circuit
periods.local_contextualization_notes: AC electricity: the power supplied by LEC to homes and businesses, why voltage stabilizers are needed. Electronics: how mobile phones, radios, and televisions work. Diodes: LED lights increasingly used in solar-powered lanterns. Transistors: the building blocks of all electronic devices. Relate semiconductor technology to the growing tech ecosystem in Monrovia.

specific_objectives: Examine the properties of an electron
Analyze the effect of radioactive substances on the human body
Draw and analyze the typical atom and the applications of nuclear energy
Distinguish between fission and fusion
content_outline: Types of nuclear reactions and the uses of nuclear energy
Radioactivity: types, radioactive substances and their effects, radioactive decay and half-life
Thermionic and photoelectric emissions: cathode rays, X-rays
suggested_activities: Discuss the existence and strength of radioactive detectors and radioactive substances
Discuss fission and fusion reactions (e.g., U-238 disintegration)
List applications of nuclear energy
Draw and display a simple atom performing tracer experiment
State and discuss effects of radioactive substances on lives
Explain cathode rays and X-rays: their nature, properties and characteristics
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Nuclear physics: X-rays at JFK Medical Center and other hospitals, the global debate about nuclear energy. Radioactivity: why radiation can be dangerous (link to health education), carbon dating and archaeology. Applications: nuclear medicine, smoke detectors. Discuss the responsible use of nuclear technology and its relevance to Africa's energy future.

specific_objectives: Examine the uncertainty principle
Analyze the principle quantum numbers and describe the motion of an electron
Discuss various types of particle accelerators
Identify and discuss the four basic interactions between particles of matter
Analyze the conservation laws of particle physics
suggested_activities: Half-life experiment using Geiger counters
Electron configuration of some light elements
Drawing and labeling diagrams of various particle accelerators
Preparing a chart of subatomic particles
periods.local_contextualization_notes: Quantum mechanics: the fundamental science behind modern technology (phones, solar panels, computers). Photoelectric effect: how solar panels convert sunlight to electricity (directly relevant to Liberia's growing solar infrastructure). Particle accelerators: the cutting-edge research happening globally. Encourage students to see themselves as future scientists who could contribute to these fields.
